# Compile from `requirements.in`

Use a `requirements.in` file when you have an application (not a library), the project does not need a
`pyproject.toml`, or you want a free-form list of dependencies independent of a build system.

The format is identical to `pip`'s requirements-file syntax. Anything `pip install -r` accepts works.

## A minimal `requirements.in`

```text
django
requests
```

Run `pip-compile`. With no positional argument, it picks up `requirements.in` in the current directory:

```console
pip-compile
```

Output (in `requirements.txt`):

```text
#
# This file is autogenerated by pip-compile with Python 3.13
# by the following command:
#
#    pip-compile
#
asgiref==3.7.2
    # via django
certifi==2024.2.2
    # via requests
charset-normalizer==3.3.2
    # via requests
django==4.2.7
    # via -r requirements.in
idna==3.6
    # via requests
requests==2.31.0
    # via -r requirements.in
sqlparse==0.4.4
    # via django
urllib3==2.2.1
    # via requests
```

## Specifier syntax

Anything PEP 508 understands works:

```text
django>=4.2,<5.0
requests~=2.31
flask==3.0.*
psycopg2-binary; sys_platform == "linux"
```

PEP 508 environment markers (`sys_platform`, `python_version`, etc.) evaluate at resolve time, on the
machine that ran `pip-compile`. See {doc}`/explanation/cross-environment`.

## Pulling in other files

Recursive include (`-r`) and constraints (`-c`) work the same as in `pip`:

```text
-r base-requirements.in
-c constraints.txt
django
```

`-r` is "include these requirements as if they were in this file". `-c` is "if any of these packages get
pulled in, use these versions". See {doc}`layered-requirements` for the layered-files pattern.

## Custom indexes and find-links

Any pip option that controls the index works here too:

```text
--index-url https://pypi.org/simple
--extra-index-url https://my-private-index.example.com/simple
--find-links https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu118
--trusted-host my-private-index.example.com

torch
django
```

The options propagate into the output unless you pass `--no-emit-index-url`, `--no-emit-find-links`, or
`--no-emit-trusted-host`. See {doc}`customize-output` and {doc}`private-indexes`.

## VCS, URL, and editable requirements

Direct VCS references work:

```text
git+https://github.com/django/django.git@main#egg=django
```

URL requirements work:

```text
django @ https://github.com/django/django/archive/4.2.7.zip
```

Editable installs work (the project at the path appears as `-e <path>` in the output, unpinned):

```text
-e ./local-package
```

The output lockfile preserves these as-is. `pip install -r requirements.txt` can replay them.

## Multiple inputs

`pip-compile` accepts multiple inputs:

```console
pip-compile -o all.txt a.in b.in c.in
```

The constraint pool is the union of all three. With multiple inputs, `--output-file` is required.

```{seealso}
- {doc}`layered-requirements` for shared pins between `requirements.in` files.
- {doc}`update-dependencies` for bumping versions on subsequent compiles.
- {doc}`use-hashes` for adding hashes to the output.
```
